meanings.definition: Relating to or marking a period of one hundred years.
examples: The town held a centenary celebration to mark its hundredth anniversary.
A centenary edition of the novel was published in 2020.

meanings.definition: Lacking the skill or ability needed to do something successfully.
examples: The incompetent manager kept losing important paperwork.
He was fired for being completely incompetent at his job.

meanings.definition: A person who lacks the ability to do something properly.
examples: The project failed because it was run by incompetents.

meanings.definition: Causing intense grief, sadness, or distress.
examples: The refugees told heartrending stories of losing their homes.
It was heartrending to watch the family say goodbye.

meanings.definition: Idealistic and impractical to an unrealistic or extravagant degree.
examples: His quixotic plan to end world hunger overnight was doomed to fail.
She had a quixotic faith that everyone was fundamentally good.

meanings.definition: Capable of making mistakes or being wrong.
examples: Even the best doctors are fallible and occasionally misdiagnose patients.
Human memory is notoriously fallible.

meanings.definition: A person believed to be able to foretell the future or perceive things beyond ordinary sight.
examples: The ancient seer warned the king of an approaching disaster.
People traveled far to consult the village seer.

meanings.definition: The thin outer layer of the skin.
examples: Sunburn damages the cells of the epidermis.
The epidermis protects the deeper layers of tissue beneath it.

meanings.definition: To accept or admit the truth, existence, or validity of something.
examples: She refused to acknowledge that she had made a mistake.
The company acknowledged the fault in its product.

meanings.definition: To show that one has noticed or recognized someone or something.
examples: He acknowledged the applause with a small bow.
She nodded to acknowledge her old friend across the room.

meanings.definition: Military weapons, ammunition, and related equipment.
examples: The soldiers stockpiled ordnance ahead of the battle.
Unexploded ordnance still litters the old battlefield.

meanings.definition: The general shape, structure, or arrangement of something.
examples: The horse had an excellent conformation for racing.
Scientists studied the conformation of the protein molecule.

meanings.definition: Easily deceived or tricked because of being too trusting.
examples: The gullible tourist paid far too much for the fake painting.
Scammers prey on gullible people online.

meanings.definition: A substance that counteracts the effects of a poison.
examples: Doctors administered the antidote before the venom could spread.
There is no known antidote for some snake bites.

meanings.definition: Something that counteracts or relieves an unpleasant situation or feeling.
examples: Laughter is a good antidote to stress.

meanings.definition: To control the functioning of a machine, process, or system.
examples: She knows how to operate the heavy machinery.
The factory operates around the clock.

meanings.definition: To perform surgery on a patient.
examples: The surgeon will operate on his knee tomorrow.

meanings.definition: Having a noticeable smell, especially a pleasant or fragrant one.
examples: The garden was filled with odorous blossoms.
An odorous wave of fresh bread drifted from the bakery.

meanings.definition: Warmth, friendliness, and kindness of character.
examples: The host welcomed his guests with great geniality.
Her geniality put everyone at ease.

meanings.definition: To hold a discussion, especially with an enemy or opponent, in order to settle a dispute.
examples: The two commanders agreed to parley before the battle resumed.
They parleyed for hours but reached no agreement.

meanings.definition: A conference or discussion between opposing sides.
examples: A flag of truce was raised to request a parley.

meanings.definition: A member of a bomber crew responsible for aiming and releasing bombs.
examples: The bombardier released the payload over the target.
As bombardier, he had to calculate the precise drop point.

meanings.definition: To arrest or capture someone, especially for a crime.
examples: Police apprehended the suspect near the border.
The thief was apprehended as he tried to flee.

meanings.definition: To understand or grasp the meaning of something.
examples: She quickly apprehended the seriousness of the situation.

meanings.definition: A small, light boat usually propelled by oars.
examples: They rowed the skiff across the calm lake.
An old fisherman tied his skiff to the dock.

meanings.definition: Steady persistence in a course of action despite difficulty or delay.
examples: Her perseverance finally paid off when she passed the exam.
Success in research demands patience and perseverance.

meanings.definition: A substance that destroys or wears away material gradually through chemical action.
examples: Acid is a powerful corrosive that can eat through metal.
Workers handle the corrosive with protective gloves.

meanings.definition: Tending to gradually destroy or wear away something.
examples: The corrosive effects of jealousy ruined their friendship.

meanings.definition: A slender, graceful young woman or girl.
examples: The dancer moved across the stage like a sylph.
She was a delicate sylph in a flowing white gown.

meanings.definition: The act of submitting or presenting something for consideration or approval.
examples: The submittal of the proposal must be completed by Friday.
The contractor prepared a submittal for the architect to review.

meanings.definition: To pay someone for work, services, or losses.
examples: The company will remunerate employees for any travel expenses.
She was generously remunerated for her consulting work.

meanings.definition: The killing of a king or sovereign.
examples: The revolution culminated in the regicide of the monarch.
The conspirators were executed for regicide.

meanings.definition: The means of maintaining life with the minimum food and resources needed to survive.
examples: The farmers depended on the land for their subsistence.
They earned barely enough for subsistence.

meanings.definition: Polite, refined, and respectable, often in an affected or self-conscious way.
examples: She spoke in a genteel manner befitting a duchess.
The family clung to their genteel traditions despite their fading fortune.

meanings.definition: A small ornamental object valued more for decoration than for use.
examples: Her shelves were crowded with knickknacks from her travels.
He bought a little knickknack as a souvenir.

meanings.definition: Fair and impartial to everyone involved.
examples: They reached an equitable division of the property.
The policy aims to ensure equitable treatment for all employees.

meanings.definition: Pleasing in appearance; attractive.
examples: She was a comely young woman with bright eyes.
The comely garden drew admiring glances from passersby.

meanings.definition: Of or relating to the ear or the sense of hearing.
examples: The doctor examined the patient for an aural infection.
Musicians develop strong aural skills.

meanings.definition: Mockery or scornful ridicule.
examples: His wild idea was met with derision by the committee.
She endured the derision of her classmates.

meanings.definition: Causing or involving great pain or suffering.
examples: The long, hot march was a torturous ordeal.
She endured a torturous wait for the test results.

meanings.definition: Serving to point out or indicate something.
examples: A rising fever is indicant of infection.
The chart includes several indicant markers of economic health.

meanings.definition: Something that points out or indicates.
examples: A sharp drop in sales is an indicant of trouble ahead.

meanings.definition: An elementary book that teaches the basics of a subject, especially reading.
examples: She learned her letters from an old reading primer.
The guide serves as a primer on personal finance.

meanings.definition: Inflicting or intended to inflict punishment; punitive.
examples: The vindicatory measures were meant to deter future offenders.
He spoke of justice in vindicatory terms.

meanings.definition: A person who performs good deeds and fulfills moral and social duties.
examples: She was known throughout the village as a tireless well-doer.
The charity honored the well-doers of the community.

meanings.definition: Something inherited from previous generations, such as traditions, culture, or property.
examples: The festival celebrates the country's rich cultural heritage.
She is proud of her Irish heritage.

meanings.definition: A person who boasts excessively about themselves.
examples: The braggart claimed he could beat anyone at chess.
Nobody believed the braggart's tall tales.

meanings.definition: The farthest or outermost point of something.
examples: A lighthouse stood at the extremity of the peninsula.
Numbness spread to the extremity of his fingers.

meanings.definition: A condition of severe difficulty, danger, or need.
examples: In their extremity, the survivors burned furniture for warmth.

meanings.definition: Extremely wicked, cruel, or shocking.
examples: The soldiers were accused of atrocious war crimes.
The dictator committed atrocious acts against his own people.

meanings.definition: Of very poor quality; appalling.
examples: The food at the restaurant was atrocious.

meanings.definition: Tending to produce sleep or a trance-like state.
examples: The hypnotic rhythm of the train lulled her to sleep.
The drug has a strong hypnotic effect.

meanings.definition: Holding the attention through a fascinating, almost trance-inducing quality.
examples: The dancer moved with hypnotic grace.

meanings.definition: A gripping tool with two jaws that work on a pivot, used to hold or pull objects.
examples: He used a pair of pinchers to pull out the nail.
The pinchers gripped the wire tightly.

meanings.definition: To make someone supremely happy or blessed.
examples: The good news seemed to beatify her entire face.
A sense of peace beatified the old monk.

meanings.definition: In the Catholic Church, to declare a dead person to be among the blessed.
examples: The Pope will beatify the late nun next month.

meanings.definition: Relating to courts of law, judges, or the administration of justice.
examples: The case is now part of a judicial inquiry.
Judicial review can overturn unconstitutional laws.

meanings.definition: A decorative molding running along the top of a wall, building, or pillar.
examples: An ornate cornice ran around the ceiling of the ballroom.
Dust had gathered along the carved cornice.

meanings.definition: More desirable or suitable than another option.
examples: A quiet evening at home is preferable to a noisy party.
Walking is preferable to driving for such a short distance.

meanings.definition: A false belief held firmly despite clear evidence to the contrary.
examples: He suffered from the delusion that he was being followed.
She clung to the delusion that he would return.

meanings.definition: The opening address delivered at a graduation ceremony, especially in American colleges.
examples: She delivered the salutatory at her university's commencement.
The salutatory welcomed the assembled families and faculty.

meanings.definition: The branch of zoology that deals with fishes.
examples: He specialized in ichthyology and studied deep-sea fish.
Her course in ichthyology covered the anatomy of sharks.

meanings.definition: The achievement of a desired aim or a favorable outcome.
examples: Years of hard work led to her success.
The launch was a tremendous success.

meanings.definition: A deliberate desire to behave in an unreasonable, contrary, or unacceptable way.
examples: Out of sheer perversity, he refused the help he badly needed.
The perversity of the plan astonished everyone.

meanings.definition: To imitate someone's speech, movements, or behavior, often for amusement.
examples: The parrot can mimic human speech.
She mimicked her teacher's voice perfectly.

meanings.definition: A person or animal skilled at imitating others.
examples: He was a gifted mimic who could impersonate any celebrity.

meanings.definition: Excessive greed for wealth or possessions.
examples: His cupidity drove him to cheat his own partners.
The treasure awakened a fierce cupidity in the crew.

meanings.definition: To conceal one's true feelings or motives by pretending otherwise.
examples: He tried to dissemble his disappointment with a forced smile.
She is too honest to dissemble for long.

meanings.definition: The quality of being correct, precise, or free from error.
examples: The report's accuracy was confirmed by independent experts.
He answered every question with remarkable accuracy.

meanings.definition: Treatment that causes someone to feel shame or a loss of dignity.
examples: He suffered the indignity of being searched in public.
She refused to endure such an indignity in silence.

meanings.definition: A writing material made from the pith of a reed plant, used in ancient Egypt and Rome.
examples: The scribe wrote the decree on a sheet of papyrus.
Ancient scrolls of papyrus were found in the tomb.

meanings.definition: An indirect or passing reference to something.
examples: Her speech contained a clear allusion to the recent scandal.
The poem makes an allusion to a Greek myth.

meanings.definition: Relating to edible grains or the plants that produce them.
examples: Wheat and barley are important cereal crops.
The region's cereal harvest was abundant this year.

meanings.definition: Irritable, childish bad temper expressed in a peevish or sulky way.
examples: He stamped his foot in petulance when told to wait.
Her constant petulance wore on everyone's patience.

meanings.definition: A guard or sentry posted to keep watch and provide protection.
examples: A sentinel stood at the gate through the night.
Tall pines rose like sentinels along the ridge.

meanings.definition: The act of providing help, service, or care, especially of a religious nature.
examples: The dying man was comforted by the priest's ministration.
Through her gentle ministration, the patients slowly recovered.

meanings.definition: The quality of being complicated or having many interrelated parts.
examples: The intricacy of the clock's mechanism amazed her.
He explained the intricacies of the legal process.

meanings.definition: The scientific description and study of human arts and industries in their historical development.
examples: His work in technography traced the evolution of ancient tools.
The museum's exhibit drew on decades of technography research.

meanings.definition: A ray of moonlight.
examples: A moonbeam slipped through the curtains and lit the floor.
Silver moonbeams shimmered on the surface of the lake.

meanings.definition: Consisting of only a single cell.
examples: Bacteria are unicellular organisms.
An amoeba is a unicellular creature visible only under a microscope.

meanings.definition: Without any limit in size, extent, or amount; immeasurable.
examples: The universe may be infinite in size.
She showed infinite patience with the restless children.

meanings.definition: To legally force a person to leave a property they occupy.
examples: The landlord threatened to evict the tenants for unpaid rent.
They were evicted from their apartment last month.

meanings.definition: Money or property left to someone in a will.
examples: He received a generous legacy from his late aunt.
She used the legacy to buy a small house.

meanings.definition: Something handed down from the past or from a predecessor.
examples: The artist left behind a lasting legacy of beautiful paintings.

meanings.definition: To make late or delay something past its proper time.
examples: Heavy traffic belated the delivery, and the package arrived after dark.
The storm belated our journey by several hours.

meanings.definition: A sum of money given as a tip in return for service, beyond what is owed.
examples: She left a generous gratuity for the attentive waiter.
The restaurant adds an automatic gratuity for large parties.

meanings.definition: An expression of blame or disapproval directed at someone for a fault.
examples: The coach's sharp reproof made the player rethink his careless pass.
She accepted the reproof quietly and promised to do better.

meanings.definition: A person who sells or arranges flowers as a business.
examples: The florist arranged a stunning bouquet of lilies for the wedding.
We stopped at the florist to buy roses on the way home.

meanings.definition: A person who is a citizen of a foreign country and not naturalized where they reside.
examples: As a resident alien, he had to renew his work visa each year.
The form asked whether the applicant was a citizen or an alien.

meanings.definition: Belonging to a foreign country or differing in nature so as to seem strange.
examples: The customs of the new city felt completely alien to her.

meanings.definition: The repetition of similar vowel sounds in nearby words, especially in poetry.
examples: The line 'the rain in Spain' relies on assonance for its musical effect.
Poets use assonance to give their verses a flowing, melodic quality.

meanings.definition: A brilliant radiance or splendor of light.
examples: The effulgence of the morning sun filled the entire valley.
Her face shone with an effulgence of pure joy.

meanings.definition: Willing to accept or permit beliefs, behavior, or opinions that differ from one's own.
examples: A tolerant teacher welcomed questions from every kind of student.
They raised their children to be tolerant of other cultures.

meanings.definition: Lacking sound reasoning or contrary to the principles of clear thought.
examples: His argument was so illogical that no one could follow it.
It would be illogical to leave before the meeting even started.

meanings.definition: The act of introducing or pouring one thing into another so as to fill it with that quality.
examples: The team welcomed an infusion of fresh ideas from the new hires.
An infusion of cash kept the failing business afloat.

meanings.definition: A liquid made by steeping a substance in water to extract its flavor or properties.
examples: She sipped a soothing infusion of chamomile before bed.

meanings.definition: A solid shape that is nearly but not perfectly spherical.
examples: The Earth is technically a spheroid, slightly flattened at the poles.
The artist molded the clay into a smooth spheroid.

meanings.definition: A large bundle of goods tightly bound for storage or transport.
examples: The farmer stacked each bale of hay in the barn.
Bales of cotton were loaded onto the freight train.

meanings.definition: To instill or introduce a quality, principle, or feeling into someone or something.
examples: The new director infused the team with fresh energy and purpose.
She tried to infuse her students with a love of reading.

meanings.definition: To steep a substance in liquid to extract its flavor or properties.
examples: Let the tea infuse for five minutes before pouring.

meanings.definition: The quality of being new, clean, or recently made.
examples: The freshness of the morning air made the hike invigorating.
You could taste the freshness of the just-picked strawberries.

meanings.definition: To win over the goodwill or friendship of someone, especially after hostility.
examples: The manager tried to conciliate the angry customers with a refund.
She spoke gently to conciliate her offended friend.

meanings.definition: Relating to or arising from the exercise of the will.
examples: Choosing to forgive was a deliberate, volitive act on her part.
Grammar marks a volitive mood that expresses wishes or commands.

meanings.definition: A printed sheet or small booklet handed out to give information.
examples: Volunteers handed out leaflets advertising the charity concert.
She read the leaflet that came with the new medication.

meanings.definition: A small or young leaf, or one of the divisions of a compound leaf.
examples: Each fern frond is made up of many delicate leaflets.

meanings.definition: To draw someone or something into a complicated or entangling situation.
examples: He didn't want to involve his family in the dispute.
The scandal involved several high-ranking officials.

meanings.definition: To have or include something as a necessary part or consequence.
examples: The job involves a lot of travel and long hours.

meanings.definition: Able to perceive or feel things through the senses.
examples: The novel imagines a future where machines become sentient.
Many argue that all sentient creatures deserve humane treatment.

meanings.definition: A feeling of physical or mental unease or mild distress.
examples: The tight shoes caused her considerable discomfort all day.
He felt a moment of discomfort when asked about his grades.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that takes the place of or acts in place of another.
examples: After his father died, his uncle became a surrogate parent.
The teacher served as a surrogate for the absent principal.

meanings.definition: The act of denying or asserting that something is false or does not exist.
examples: His silence felt like a negation of everything they had agreed on.
The word 'not' signals negation in an English sentence.

meanings.definition: A sleeveless garment, a vest, worn over a shirt and under a jacket.
examples: He wore a silk waistcoat beneath his tailored suit.
The butler's uniform included a neatly buttoned waistcoat.

meanings.definition: A small state or territory ruled by a prince.
examples: Monaco is a tiny principality on the French Riviera.
The novel is set in an imaginary mountain principality.

meanings.definition: To begin a new venture, project, or course of action.
examples: They embarked on an ambitious plan to rebuild the school.
After graduation she embarked on a career in journalism.

meanings.definition: To board a ship or aircraft at the start of a journey.
examples: The passengers embarked at dawn for the long voyage.

meanings.definition: Open to bribery or willing to act dishonestly in return for money.
examples: The venal official accepted bribes to ignore the violations.
A venal press will print anything for the right price.

meanings.definition: A young child who is mischievous, poor, or raggedly dressed.
examples: A grinning urchin darted through the crowded market.
The street urchins begged for coins outside the theater.

meanings.definition: Something unnaturally huge, distorted, or hideously ugly.
examples: The new tower was a concrete monstrosity that ruined the skyline.
Critics called the sculpture a monstrosity of tangled metal.

meanings.definition: To lessen the seriousness or apparent guilt of something by offering excuses.
examples: Nothing could extenuate his deliberate dishonesty.
The lawyer cited his client's youth to extenuate the offense.

meanings.definition: The person who steers a rowing boat and directs the crew.
examples: The coxswain shouted the stroke rate to keep the rowers in time.
As coxswain, she steered the eight to a narrow victory.

meanings.definition: A group of five performers, or a musical piece written for five voices or instruments.
examples: The string quintet performed a lively piece by Mozart.
Their jazz quintet played to a packed club on Friday night.

meanings.definition: Based on or showing faith, trust, or confidence; taken as a fixed point of reference.
examples: Surveyors aligned the instrument with a fiducial mark on the map.
The treaty rested on a fiducial bond between the two nations.

meanings.definition: The act of yielding to the power, authority, or wishes of another.
examples: The rebels were finally beaten into submission.
Her quiet submission to every demand surprised her friends.

meanings.definition: Something formally presented for consideration or judgment.
examples: The deadline for contest submissions is next Friday.

meanings.definition: To make a law, doctrine, or idea widely known by official proclamation.
examples: The government promulgated new safety regulations last month.
Missionaries traveled far to promulgate their beliefs.

meanings.definition: A person's legal declaration of how their property is to be distributed after death; a will.
examples: In his last will and testament he left the house to his niece.
The estate was divided exactly as the testament directed.

meanings.definition: Something that serves as proof or evidence of a quality or fact.
examples: The bridge is a testament to the engineers' skill.

meanings.definition: A sharp, shrill cry or scream caused by fear, pain, or surprise.
examples: A shriek of terror echoed through the dark house.
The children let out a shriek of delight when the clown appeared.

meanings.definition: So thin and delicate as to be nearly transparent.
examples: She wore a diaphanous veil that floated in the breeze.
Diaphanous curtains let the soft light fill the room.

meanings.definition: Inclined to follow another without independent thought; readily led.
examples: The sequacious crowd cheered whatever the speaker said.
He was too sequacious to ever question his leader's orders.

meanings.definition: A strong feeling of dislike or opposition toward something.
examples: She has a deep aversion to spiders.
His aversion to crowds kept him away from the festival.

meanings.definition: To feel intense dislike or disgust for someone or something.
examples: I absolutely loathe waiting in long lines.
He loathed the cold, damp winters of the north.

meanings.definition: The view or character of belonging to and being at home in all parts of the world rather than one nation.
examples: The city's cosmopolitanism drew artists from every continent.
Her cosmopolitanism made her comfortable in any culture.

meanings.definition: A person who supports banning the manufacture and sale of alcoholic drinks by law.
examples: The prohibitionist campaigned tirelessly to close every saloon.
As a committed prohibitionist, she lobbied for the new ban.

meanings.definition: A large, stately tomb or building housing a tomb.
examples: The emperor was buried in a marble mausoleum.
Tourists filed quietly through the grand mausoleum.

meanings.definition: Similar in nature or character; related.
examples: His love of puzzles is akin to a passion for chess.
The two languages are closely akin to each other.

meanings.definition: To make someone feel awkward, self-conscious, or ashamed.
examples: His loud joke embarrassed her in front of the whole class.
I didn't mean to embarrass you by mentioning your mistake.

meanings.definition: To protest or argue forcefully against something one disapproves of.
examples: The workers remonstrated with the manager over the pay cut.
She remonstrated against the unfair new rules.

meanings.definition: An artificial show of manner or feeling meant to impress others.
examples: His upper-class accent was pure affectation.
She spoke without any affectation, simply and honestly.

meanings.definition: A skilled worker who operates machines or makes and repairs metal parts.
examples: The machinist cut each part to exact specifications.
He trained for years to become a precision machinist.

meanings.definition: Having enough wealth to live comfortably; prosperous.
examples: They came from a well-to-do family with a country estate.
Only the well-to-do could afford tickets to the gala.

meanings.definition: A beginner or novice with little skill or experience in a field.
examples: As a tyro at chess, he lost his first dozen games.
The workshop welcomes both experts and tyros alike.

meanings.definition: Unable to be touched or grasped physically; not having a physical presence.
examples: Goodwill is an intangible asset that is hard to value.
There was an intangible tension in the air.

meanings.definition: A state of extreme physical or mental tiredness.
examples: After the marathon she collapsed from sheer exhaustion.
Days of overwork drove him to complete exhaustion.

meanings.definition: The complete using up of a resource or supply.
examples: The exhaustion of the oil reserves forced the town to seek new energy.

meanings.definition: One of the two upper chambers of the heart that receives blood from the veins.
examples: Blood flows into the right auricle before entering the ventricle.
The doctor explained how each auricle of the heart functions.

meanings.definition: The visible external part of the ear.
examples: A piercing was placed at the top of the auricle.

meanings.definition: An account of a person's life written by that person.
examples: She published her autobiography at the age of seventy.
His autobiography recounts his rise from poverty to fame.

meanings.definition: Opposed to the practice of human slavery.
examples: The newspaper took a firm antislavery stance.
Antislavery activists organized protests across the country.

meanings.definition: A person or organization that puts money into something expecting a profit.
examples: The startup attracted several wealthy investors.
As a cautious investor, she preferred safe government bonds.

meanings.definition: To destroy or drastically reduce a large portion of something.
examples: The drought decimated the region's wheat crop.
A single epidemic decimated the small village.

meanings.definition: Giving off bubbles of gas, as a fizzy liquid.
examples: The effervescent soda fizzed over the rim of the glass.
She dropped an effervescent tablet into the water.

meanings.definition: Lively, enthusiastic, and full of high spirits.
examples: Her effervescent personality lit up the whole room.

meanings.definition: To dig up and replant something in a different place.
examples: We transplanted the young saplings to the back garden.
She carefully transplanted the seedlings into larger pots.

meanings.definition: To transfer an organ or tissue from one body or part to another.
examples: Surgeons worked through the night to transplant the donor heart.

meanings.definition: To bring something up to date with present-day styles, methods, or technology.
examples: The company spent millions to modernize its aging factories.
They modernized the old kitchen with new appliances.

meanings.definition: A deep, gaping opening or split in the earth's surface.
examples: A narrow bridge spanned the rocky chasm.
They peered nervously into the dark chasm below.

meanings.definition: A wide difference or gap between people or ideas.
examples: A growing chasm divided the two political factions.

meanings.definition: A meadow or grassy field.
examples: Cattle grazed peacefully in the lush green mead.
Wildflowers dotted the open mead in spring.

meanings.definition: An alcoholic drink made from fermented honey and water.
examples: The guests raised their cups of mead at the feast.

meanings.definition: To form the basis, foundation, or hidden cause of something.
examples: Deep resentment underlay his calm reply.
A simple principle underlies the entire theory.

meanings.definition: Impressive or grand in style, often more than is justified or sincere.
examples: He had grandiose plans to build a palace by the sea.
Her grandiose promises rarely came to anything.

meanings.definition: To be similar to or correspond closely with something else.
examples: His career paralleled that of his famous father.
The novel's plot parallels real events from the war.

meanings.definition: To gradually recover one's health after an illness or injury.
examples: She went to the seaside to convalesce after surgery.
He spent weeks convalescing in a quiet country home.

meanings.definition: Elaborately or heavily decorated.
examples: The palace ceiling was covered in ornate gold carvings.
She wore an ornate necklace studded with jewels.

meanings.definition: Eager to learn or know things, often by asking many questions.
examples: The inquisitive child asked why the sky was blue.
Her inquisitive mind led her into scientific research.

meanings.definition: A figure of speech that describes one thing as if it were another to suggest a likeness.
examples: Calling time a thief is a common metaphor.
The poem uses the sea as a metaphor for endless longing.

meanings.definition: A special or important event, time, or celebration.
examples: Their wedding was a joyous occasion for the whole family.
She saved the dress for a special occasion.

meanings.definition: A reason or cause for something, or the right moment for it.
examples: His remark gave her occasion to leave early.

meanings.definition: A preliminary memorandum or draft agreement between parties, less formal and binding than a full treaty.
examples: The diplomats signed a protocol outlining the terms they would later expand into a formal treaty.

meanings.definition: The accepted system of rules governing formal or official conduct and ceremony.
examples: Strict protocol dictated who would be seated nearest the ambassador at dinner.

meanings.definition: A set of standardized rules that allow electronic devices to communicate with one another.
examples: The two servers exchanged data using a secure communication protocol.

meanings.definition: A helpful or advantageous result of something.
examples: Regular exercise offers the benefit of improved health.
One major benefit of the new policy is shorter wait times.

meanings.definition: To receive an advantage or to provide an advantage to someone.
examples: Students benefit from smaller class sizes.

meanings.definition: A tendency to keep silent or to be reserved in expressing one's thoughts and feelings.
examples: Her natural reticence made her reluctant to share details about her private life.

meanings.definition: A person who murders someone, especially a prominent figure, by treacherous or secret means.
examples: The assassin slipped through the crowd and vanished before anyone realized what had happened.

meanings.definition: The right or privilege of voting in political elections.
examples: The movement fought for decades to win universal suffrage for all adult citizens.

meanings.definition: Complete and absolute; nothing other than.
examples: Winning the lottery was a matter of sheer luck.

meanings.definition: Of a fabric so fine that it is almost transparent.
examples: She wore a dress made of sheer silk.

meanings.definition: Extremely steep, rising or dropping almost vertically.
examples: The climbers gazed up at the sheer cliff face.

meanings.definition: The lowest singing voice or part for a woman or boy, below the soprano.
examples: She sang alto in the church choir.

meanings.definition: To deliberately gain favor or approval from someone, often by flattery or eager attentiveness.
examples: He tried to ingratiate himself with the new manager by praising her every decision.

meanings.definition: To think about something again, especially with a view to changing a previous decision.
examples: The committee agreed to reconsider its ruling after hearing the new evidence.

meanings.definition: Growing thickly and abundantly; lush.
examples: The garden was full of luxuriant green vines after the rainy season.

meanings.definition: Rich, elaborate, or extravagant in detail or style.
examples: The novel is written in a luxuriant, ornate prose.

meanings.definition: Entirely; to the full or complete extent.
examples: The decision is wholly yours to make.
She was wholly unaware of the danger ahead.

meanings.definition: Deliberate betrayal of trust; treachery.
examples: His perfidy was revealed when he sold his partner's secrets to a rival firm.

meanings.definition: To live or spend time in a dull, inactive way, with little mental or physical effort.
examples: After losing his job, he spent weeks vegetating in front of the television.

meanings.definition: Of a plant, to grow or sprout.
examples: The seeds began to vegetate once the soil warmed.

meanings.definition: To force someone out of a position, office, or place.
examples: The board voted to oust the chief executive after the scandal.

meanings.definition: A literary work made up of ten parts or books, especially Boccaccio's collection of one hundred tales told over ten days.
examples: The students read several stories from the Decameron in their literature course.

meanings.definition: Lasting or intended to last for a long time without changing; not temporary.
examples: The accident left him with a permanent scar.
They finally found a permanent home after years of moving.

meanings.definition: Familiar with or knowledgeable about a subject.
examples: She is thoroughly conversant with the latest research in her field.

meanings.definition: Marked by restraint or moderation, especially in eating and drinking.
examples: He led an abstemious life, rarely indulging in rich food or wine.

meanings.definition: A weight, typically of lead, attached to a line for measuring depth or checking that something is vertical.
examples: The builder used a plummet to make sure the wall was perfectly upright.

meanings.definition: To fall or drop straight down very rapidly.
examples: Stock prices plummeted after the disappointing earnings report.

meanings.definition: To act as a friend to someone, especially by offering help or support when it is needed.
examples: She befriended the new student who seemed lonely and lost.

meanings.definition: The process of memorizing through mechanical repetition, with little attention to meaning.
examples: The children learned the multiplication tables by rote.

meanings.definition: Reasonably good but not excellent; acceptable.
examples: The food at the diner was tolerable, though nothing special.

meanings.definition: Able to be endured or put up with.
examples: The pain was tolerable once she took the medicine.

meanings.definition: A brimless, cone-shaped felt cap, usually red with a black tassel.
examples: The old man wore a bright red fez as he sat outside the café.

meanings.definition: The quality of having traits traditionally regarded as feminine, especially in a man.
examples: The character's effeminacy was exaggerated for comic effect in the play.

meanings.definition: A person's appearance, bearing, or manner, especially as an indication of mood or character.
examples: The judge had a stern mien that commanded respect.

meanings.definition: Skilled at handling people and situations with tact and sensitivity.
examples: She gave a diplomatic answer that avoided offending either side.

meanings.definition: Relating to diplomacy or the management of relations between countries.
examples: The two nations resumed diplomatic relations after years of tension.

meanings.definition: The band of colors produced when light is separated by passing through a prism or by other means.
examples: A rainbow displays the full spectrum of visible light.

meanings.definition: A continuous range over which something, such as opinions or qualities, may vary.
examples: Their views covered the entire political spectrum.

meanings.definition: To hold back or restrain a feeling, action, or impulse.
examples: She struggled to repress a laugh during the solemn ceremony.

meanings.definition: To subdue or put down by force, as a rebellion or protest.
examples: The regime repressed all forms of public dissent.

meanings.definition: A title of courtesy or rank, traditionally placed after a man's name, and now often used for attorneys.
examples: The letter was addressed to John Smith, Esquire.

meanings.definition: The using up or gradual destruction of a resource or material.
examples: The factory's high consumption of water strained the local supply.

meanings.definition: The act of eating, drinking, or otherwise using goods.
examples: The report measured the country's consumption of sugar per person.

meanings.definition: To feel intense hatred or loathing for something.
examples: She abominated cruelty toward animals in any form.

meanings.definition: To refer to or mention something as an example, proof, or authority.
examples: The author cited several studies to support her argument.

meanings.definition: To formally summon someone to appear in court or to commend them officially.
examples: The officer cited the driver for speeding.

meanings.definition: Impossible to understand.
examples: His handwriting was so messy that the note was completely incomprehensible.

meanings.definition: A person who falsely claims to have special knowledge or skill; a fraud or quack.
examples: The so-called healer turned out to be a charlatan selling worthless potions.

meanings.definition: Serving as a protector or guardian.
examples: The ancient city had a tutelar god believed to watch over its people.

meanings.definition: A person who physically attacks another.
examples: The victim was unable to identify her assailant in the dark.

meanings.definition: Able to be drawn out or hammered into a thin wire or thread without breaking.
examples: Copper is highly ductile, which is why it is used for electrical wiring.

meanings.definition: A record or account of the line of descent of a person or family from their ancestors.
examples: She traced her family's genealogy back five generations.

meanings.definition: Relating to a navy, warships, or sailors.
examples: The country expanded its naval forces to protect its coastline.

meanings.definition: A person who makes, sells, or repairs goods made of fur.
examples: The furrier carefully repaired the lining of her old fur coat.

meanings.definition: A long and complicated, often pointless, procedure or set of instructions.
examples: Getting the permit involved a tedious rigmarole of forms and signatures.

meanings.definition: Confused or meaningless talk; nonsense.
examples: He gave some rigmarole about traffic to excuse his lateness.

meanings.definition: The act of confirming that something is true, accurate, or valid.
examples: The bank requires verification of your identity before opening an account.

meanings.definition: A priest who hears confessions and gives spiritual guidance.
examples: The king sought advice from his trusted confessor before making the decision.

meanings.definition: Scornful or contemptuous ridicule directed at someone or something.
examples: His clumsy attempt at dancing drew mockery from the crowd.

meanings.definition: An absurdly inadequate or insulting imitation of something.
examples: The unfair trial was a mockery of justice.

meanings.definition: To draw out or bring forth something latent or potential.
examples: A skilled teacher can educe ideas the students didn't know they had.

meanings.definition: Mercy or leniency shown toward someone, especially an offender.
examples: The governor granted clemency and reduced the prisoner's sentence.

meanings.definition: Mildness, especially of weather.
examples: The unexpected clemency of the winter allowed the crops to survive.

meanings.definition: To seize hold of someone or something and struggle with them.
examples: The wrestler grappled with his opponent for control of the match.

meanings.definition: To try hard to deal with or understand a difficult problem.
examples: Scientists are still grappling with the causes of the disease.

meanings.definition: The process or state of becoming outdated and no longer in use.
examples: The rapid pace of technology pushes many gadgets into obsolescence within a few years.

meanings.definition: A staff carried as a symbol of office or authority, or used as a weapon.
examples: The field marshal carried a gold-tipped baton as a sign of his rank.

meanings.definition: A thin stick used by a conductor to direct an orchestra, or passed between runners in a relay race.
examples: The conductor raised his baton and the orchestra fell silent.

meanings.definition: To swell or cause to swell outward in all directions from internal pressure.
examples: The balloon distended as it filled with air.

meanings.definition: Sad, discouraged, or lacking in energy and cheerfulness.
examples: She felt low-spirited after the long, gray winter.

meanings.definition: To give someone fresh energy, strength, or liveliness.
examples: A brisk morning walk never fails to invigorate me.

meanings.definition: In its original, unspoiled condition; perfectly clean and fresh.
examples: The hikers reached a pristine lake untouched by development.

meanings.definition: Belonging to the earliest or original state of something.
examples: Geologists studied the rock for clues about the planet's pristine past.

meanings.definition: Useful and able to give good service over time; durable and practical.
examples: The old truck was plain but serviceable, getting them where they needed to go.

meanings.definition: To accept that something is true or that someone is telling the truth.
examples: I believe what she told me about the accident.

meanings.definition: To hold an opinion or to have religious faith.
examples: He believes in working hard and being honest.

meanings.definition: A person who refuses to believe something or who lacks religious faith.
examples: As a lifelong disbeliever, he doubted every miraculous claim he heard.

meanings.definition: To retire someone from service, or to become worn out or unfit through age or long use.
examples: The aging machinery had superannuated and was finally replaced.

meanings.definition: To dismiss or discard something as old-fashioned or obsolete.
examples: The new policy superannuated the old paper-based filing system.

meanings.definition: An instrument for making precise measurements of very small distances, angles, or dimensions.
examples: The engineer used a micrometer to measure the thickness of the metal sheet.

meanings.definition: Able to be hammered, pressed, or shaped without breaking or cracking.
examples: Gold is so malleable that it can be beaten into extremely thin sheets.

meanings.definition: Easily influenced, controlled, or changed.
examples: Young children have malleable minds that absorb new ideas quickly.

meanings.definition: A container in which sacred relics are kept and displayed.
examples: The cathedral's jeweled reliquary was said to hold a saint's bone.

meanings.definition: Funeral rites or ceremonies held to honor a dead person.
examples: Thousands attended the obsequies of the beloved leader.

meanings.definition: Unwilling and hesitant to do something.
examples: He was reluctant to admit that he had made a mistake.

meanings.definition: Showing dry, mocking, or ironic humor.
examples: She gave a wry smile at the absurdity of the situation.

meanings.definition: Twisted or bent to one side, especially the face or features.
examples: He made a wry face when he tasted the sour lemon.

meanings.definition: The large country house and surrounding landed estate of a lord or nobleman.
examples: The wealthy family lived in a sprawling manor surrounded by farmland.

meanings.definition: Sworn testimony taken in writing outside of court for use as legal evidence.
examples: The witness gave a lengthy deposition before the trial began.

meanings.definition: The act of removing someone from a position of power or authority.
examples: The deposition of the king marked the end of the dynasty.

meanings.definition: Impossible to understand or interpret; mysterious.
examples: Her inscrutable expression gave no hint of what she was thinking.

meanings.definition: A medieval knight who traveled in search of adventures and chivalrous deeds.
examples: The story follows a knight errant who rescues travelers from bandits.

meanings.definition: A greater liking for one thing or person over another.
examples: She has a strong preference for tea over coffee.

meanings.definition: A thing chosen or favored above others.
examples: My first preference for the trip would be the mountains.

meanings.definition: Showing strong, intense, or passionate feeling.
examples: She made a vehement protest against the unfair decision.

meanings.definition: A thickened, hardened area of skin; a callus.
examples: Years of manual labor had left callosities on his palms.

meanings.definition: A lack of feeling or sensitivity; hard-heartedness.
examples: The callosity with which he dismissed their suffering shocked everyone.

meanings.definition: To extend on all sides of something; to encircle.
examples: Tall hedges surround the garden on every side.
Police surrounded the building before going in.

meanings.definition: Excessively theatrical or artificial in manner.
examples: Her stagy gestures made the apology seem insincere.
He spoke in a stagy whisper, as if performing for an audience.

meanings.definition: To replace one thing, especially a penalty, with another that is less severe.
examples: The governor commuted his death sentence to life in prison.
Officials agreed to commute the fine to community service.

meanings.definition: To travel regularly between one's home and place of work.
examples: She commutes from the suburbs to the city each morning.

meanings.definition: Differing or inconsistent; at odds with something else.
examples: The two witnesses gave discrepant accounts of the accident.
Auditors flagged the discrepant figures in the report.

meanings.definition: To burn something slightly or only on the surface.
examples: She singed her sleeve as she reached over the candle.
The flames singed the edges of the curtains.

meanings.definition: Relating to the medieval system of holding land in exchange for service between lords and vassals.
examples: Under the feudal system, peasants worked the land owned by their lord.
The novel is set in a feudal society ruled by warring barons.

meanings.definition: The part of the skull that encloses and protects the brain.
examples: The X-ray showed a small fracture in the cranium.
The helmet is designed to shield the cranium during a fall.

meanings.definition: A forceful and bitter verbal attack or criticism.
examples: He launched into a long diatribe against the new policy.
Her column was a diatribe denouncing corruption in city hall.

meanings.definition: A complicated network of passages or paths in which it is easy to get lost; a maze.
examples: We wandered through the labyrinth of narrow streets in the old town.
The cave system is a labyrinth of tunnels and chambers.

meanings.definition: Something extremely complex or confusing in structure.
examples: Filing the claim meant navigating a labyrinth of paperwork.

meanings.definition: The property or money a bride brings to her husband at marriage.
examples: Her family offered a generous dowry of land and jewelry.
In some cultures the custom of paying a dowry still persists.

meanings.definition: Perceiving or feeling something in advance; having a foreboding.
examples: She had a presentient unease the night before the storm hit.
His presentient dread proved justified when the news arrived.

meanings.definition: To wear away the edge of a fabric or rope so that loose threads form.
examples: The hem of his jeans had begun to fray.
Years of use had frayed the rope until it nearly snapped.

meanings.definition: To strain or wear down, as one's nerves or temper.
examples: The long delay frayed everyone's patience.

meanings.definition: The state of abstaining from immoral sexual conduct; moral or sexual purity.
examples: The order required its members to take a vow of chastity.
Her chastity was prized in the strict society of the time.

meanings.definition: A stopping or ending of an action or process.
examples: Both sides agreed to a cessation of hostilities.
The sudden cessation of the noise left an eerie silence.

meanings.definition: A person who lives by begging.
examples: A mendicant sat at the temple gate asking for alms.
The friars lived as mendicants, owning nothing of their own.

meanings.definition: Depending on charity or begging for a living.
examples: He belonged to a mendicant religious order.

meanings.definition: Not genuine, authentic, or true; false.
examples: The painting turned out to be a spurious copy.
He was arrested on spurious charges that were later dropped.

meanings.definition: Made up of parts or elements that are different in kind.
examples: The city has a heterogeneous population from many cultures.
The samples were too heterogeneous to compare directly.

meanings.definition: Excessive or superstitious devotion to mystical or secret religious doctrines.
examples: The sect's cabalism filled its rituals with hidden meanings.
Critics dismissed his theories as little more than cabalism.

meanings.definition: A place where bees and their hives are kept.
examples: The farmer harvested honey from his small apiary.
Rows of white hives stood in the apiary behind the orchard.

meanings.definition: One of the first people to explore or settle a new region.
examples: The pioneers crossed the plains in covered wagons.
Her grandparents were pioneers who built a farm in the wilderness.

meanings.definition: A person who is among the first to develop or apply a new idea or method.
examples: She was a pioneer in the field of genetic research.

meanings.definition: A large body of infantry made up of two or more companies and forming part of a regiment.
examples: The battalion advanced toward the enemy lines at dawn.
He commanded a battalion of nearly a thousand soldiers.

meanings.definition: Intended to win goodwill or to settle a disagreement.
examples: She made a conciliatory gesture by apologizing first.
His conciliatory tone helped calm the angry crowd.

meanings.definition: To cause grief, distress, or injury to someone.
examples: The unfair ruling aggrieved many of the workers.
She felt deeply aggrieved by the false accusation.

meanings.definition: Using ridicule or irony to expose folly, vice, or corruption.
examples: The play offered a satiric look at greedy politicians.
His satiric essays mocked the fashions of high society.

meanings.definition: The use of gestures and body movement instead of speech to convey meaning.
examples: She explained the directions through pantomime when no one spoke her language.
The clown told the whole story in pantomime.

meanings.definition: A minor or insignificant king; a petty ruler.
examples: The empire was carved up among a dozen quarreling kinglings.
He was no emperor, just a kingling of a tiny mountain realm.

meanings.definition: Out of place or unsuitable for the situation, surroundings, or other elements.
examples: His casual clothes looked incongruous at the formal dinner.
There was something incongruous about the modern tower beside the old church.

meanings.definition: Confident, smooth, and pleasant in manner, sometimes in a superficial way.
examples: The suave host charmed every guest at the party.
He gave a suave reply that put the reporters at ease.

meanings.definition: Required by circumstances or necessary for a purpose.
examples: She had the requisite experience for the job.
Applicants must submit all the requisite documents.

meanings.definition: A thing that is necessary for a particular purpose.
examples: Patience is a requisite for teaching young children.

meanings.definition: To deliberately keep someone or something out or away.
examples: The club voted to exclude non-members from the event.
Certain expenses were excluded from the budget.

meanings.definition: The system or act of imposing and collecting taxes by a government.
examples: The colonists protested taxation without representation.
Higher taxation funded the new public services.

meanings.definition: A person who travels on foot.
examples: A pedestrian was waiting to cross at the corner.
The new bridge is reserved for pedestrians and cyclists.

meanings.definition: Lacking imagination or excitement; dull and ordinary.
examples: The review called the film pedestrian and forgettable.

meanings.definition: A protective covering for the leg, usually worn from ankle to knee.
examples: The hikers wore leather leggings to guard against thorns.
Each soldier was issued a pair of canvas leggings.

meanings.definition: A named group of stars that appears to form a pattern in the sky.
examples: He pointed out the constellation Orion above the trees.
Ancient sailors used the constellations to find their way.

meanings.definition: A gathering or group of related people or things.
examples: The festival drew a constellation of famous artists.

meanings.definition: To slow down or block the progress of something.
examples: Heavy snow impeded the rescue effort.
Nothing could impede her determination to succeed.

meanings.definition: A lack of interest, enthusiasm, or emotional concern.
examples: Voter apathy led to a very low turnout.
He watched the whole scandal with complete apathy.

meanings.definition: A solid landing place along a harbor where ships are loaded and unloaded.
examples: Fishing boats were tied up along the stone quay.
Cargo was stacked on the quay waiting to be shipped.

meanings.definition: The tendency or ability of something to float in a liquid or gas.
examples: The life jacket gives a swimmer extra buoyancy.
Salt water increases the buoyancy of the body.

meanings.definition: A cheerful and optimistic disposition.
examples: Her natural buoyancy lifted the spirits of the whole team.

meanings.definition: To break or undo the connection between things.
examples: Please disconnect the printer before moving it.
A storm disconnected several homes from the power grid.

meanings.definition: Extremely large in size, degree, or extent.
examples: The project required an immense amount of work.
From the summit they gazed at the immense valley below.
